bull.b: AMD's materially improved cash generation (TTM OCF ~$9.7B and FCF ~$8.6B, FCF up ~27%) provides durable funding for R&D, product ramps, capacity purchases and opportunistic investments. Sustained FCF strengthens strategic optionality and supports execution through industry cycles over the next 2–6 months.

bull.t: Data Center/AI TAM And Product Roadmap
bull.b: A materially larger server CPU TAM (> $120B by 2030) and a clear roadmap (sixth‑gen EPYC/Zen 6, AI‑optimized Verano) anchor multi‑year structural demand. This positions AMD to capture secular AI/data‑center spending and supports durable revenue and share gains across cloud and enterprise for the medium term.

Extremely low leverage (debt-to-equity ≈0.06) and ample equity provide financial flexibility to fund product ramps, absorb cyclical downturns, and make strategic investments without near‑term refinancing pressure, preserving capital structure resilience across 2–6 month planning horizons.

bear.b: The planned H2 ramp of lower‑margin Instinct GPUs (MI450/Helios) can structurally reduce blended gross margins as GPU volume scales. Unless offset by higher‑margin CPU mix or cost reductions, this margin pressure could persist across product ramps and temper profitability expansion over the next several quarters.

A higher fixed cost base from aggressive R&D and go‑to‑market spending (OpEx +42% YoY) raises the revenue threshold needed to sustain operating margins. Elevated, somewhat unpredictable OpEx can compress near‑term operating leverage if revenue growth lags, creating lasting pressure until investments scale.

Industry supply tightness and data‑center build‑out/power limitations are structural constraints that can slow shipment ramps and cause quarter‑to‑quarter variability (e.g., China step‑downs). These capacity and infrastructure bottlenecks could limit near‑term scale and sustain volatility over the next 2–6 months.
